section.title: The First Journey: Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe
section.sectionRationale: The first journey continues through the cities of Lycaonia. At Iconium the gospel divides the city; at Lystra a lifelong cripple is healed, and the pagan crowd nearly worships Paul and Barnabas before turning to stone Paul instead. Yet the apostles return through the very cities that opposed them, strengthening the young churches and appointing elders, then report to Antioch that God has opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
section.thesis: The gospel advances through both wonder and violence: it is confused with pagan worship, opposed to the point of stoning, yet it presses on as the apostles plant and strengthen churches, teaching that we enter God's kingdom through many hardships, because God Himself has opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
section.frameNote: Four movements: bold preaching and division at Iconium; the healing at Lystra and the crowd's attempt to sacrifice to the apostles; the stoning of Paul; and the return journey strengthening disciples, appointing elders, and reporting the open door at Antioch.

units.label: Bold witness and division at Iconium

verses.text: At Iconium, Paul and Barnabas went as usual into the Jewish synagogue, where they spoke so well that a great number of Jews and Greeks believed.
But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers.
So Paul and Barnabas spent considerable time there, speaking boldly for the Lord, who affirmed the message of His {{charis|grace}} by enabling them to perform signs and wonders.
The people of the city were divided. Some sided with the Jews, and others with the apostles.
But when the Gentiles and Jews, together with their rulers, set out to mistreat and stone them,
they found out about it and fled to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe and to the surrounding region,
where they continued to preach the gospel.

units.label: Healing at Lystra and misplaced worship

verses.text: In Lystra there sat a man crippled in his feet, who was lame from birth and had never walked.
This man was listening to the words of Paul, who looked intently at him and saw that he had faith to be healed.
In a loud voice Paul called out, “Stand up on your feet!” And the man jumped up and began to walk.
When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voices in the Lycaonian language: “The gods have come down to us in human form!”
Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul they called Hermes, because he was the chief speaker.
The priest of Zeus, whose temple was just outside the city, brought bulls and wreaths to the city gates, hoping to offer a sacrifice along with the crowds.

units.label: Turn from idols to the living God
units.note: we are only men; turn to the living God

verses.text: But when the {{apostoloi|apostles}} Barnabas and Paul found out about this, they tore their clothes and rushed into the crowd, shouting,

“Men, why are you doing this? We too are only men, human like you. We are bringing you good news that you should turn from these worthless things to the living God, who made heaven and earth and sea and everything in them.
In past generations, He let all nations go their own way.
Yet He has not left Himself without testimony to His goodness: He gives you rain from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling your hearts with food and gladness.”
Even with these words, Paul and Barnabas could hardly stop the crowds from sacrificing to them.

units.label: The stoning of Paul
units.note: worshiped one hour, stoned the next

verses.text: Then some Jews arrived from Antioch and Iconium and won over the crowds. They stoned Paul and dragged him outside the city, presuming he was dead.
But after the disciples had gathered around him, he got up and went back into the city. And the next day he left with Barnabas for Derbe.

units.label: Strengthening the churches, the open door
units.note: many hardships, appointed elders, faith opened to the Gentiles

verses.text: They preached the gospel to that city and made many disciples. Then they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch,
strengthening the souls of the disciples and encouraging them to continue in the faith. “We must endure many hardships to enter the kingdom of God,” they said.
Paul and Barnabas appointed {{presbyteroi|elders}} for them in each church, praying and fasting as they entrusted them to the Lord, in whom they had believed.
After passing through Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia.
And when they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
From Attalia they sailed to Antioch, where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work they had just completed.
When they arrived, they gathered the church together and reported all that God had done through them, and how He had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
And they spent a long time there with the disciples.

charis.gloss: Grace, undeserved favour. The Lord confirms the message of His grace with signs and wonders; the good news the apostles preach is a word of grace, and it is to God's grace that the whole work is finally commended, verse 26.

apostoloi.gloss: One sent, an emissary, an apostle. Luke calls Barnabas and Paul apostles, sent ones, here in the plural. They refuse the worship the crowd offers and insist they are only men, sent to point away from themselves to the living God.

presbyteroi.gloss: Elder, an appointed overseer of a local church. The apostles appoint elders in every young congregation, entrusting the churches to God through ordained leaders, so that the work would be established and continue after they moved on.

basic.context: Paul and Barnabas press on through the cities of Lycaonia. At Iconium their bold preaching wins many but splits the city, and a plot to stone them drives them to Lystra and Derbe. In Lystra Paul heals a man lame from birth, and the pagan crowd, thinking the gods have come down, tries to sacrifice to them as Zeus and Hermes. The apostles are horrified and beg the people to turn from idols to the living God. Then, stirred by opponents, the same kind of crowd stones Paul and leaves him for dead, yet he rises and continues. On the way home the two return through the hostile cities to strengthen the disciples and appoint elders, and report to Antioch that God has opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
basic.hermeneutics: Watch how quickly the crowd swings from worship to violence, a warning against building faith on signs and popularity. Note Paul's approach to pure pagans in verses 15 to 17: with no shared Scripture, he appeals to creation and God's kindness in rain and harvest as a bridge to the gospel. And hear the sober realism of verse 22, that the road into the kingdom runs through many hardships, set against the triumphant note that God has opened the door.
basic.originalLanguages: Three words frame the chapter. The Lord confirms the message of His grace, charis, with signs, and the whole work is commended to God's grace. Barnabas and Paul are named apostoloi, sent ones, who refuse worship and point to God. And they appoint presbyteroi, elders, in each church, planting leadership so the young congregations will endure.
basic.typology: Paul's appeal to the living God who made heaven and earth echoes the prophets' mockery of idols and Israel's confession of the one Creator. The healing of the man lame from birth recalls the sign at the temple gate in Acts 3 and points to the wholeness Christ brings, a foretaste of the restored creation.
basic.christ: Though Christ is not named in the speech to the pagans, He is the good news the apostles bring and the Lord who confirms the word of His grace. The apostles refuse the worship due to God alone, unlike the false gods of the crowd, and unlike Herod in the previous chapter. The kingdom they preach is Christ's kingdom, entered through the same path of suffering their Master walked.
basic.god: God is the living Creator who made heaven, earth, and sea, who has never left Himself without witness, giving rain and fruitful seasons even to nations that ignored Him. He is the one who opens the door of faith to the Gentiles and to whom the whole work is entrusted. Salvation is His doing from beginning to end.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 3 records a parallel healing of a man lame from birth. Romans 1 speaks of God's testimony in creation. Acts 10 shows God first opening the door to the Gentiles through Peter. Second Timothy 3 echoes that all who live godly in Christ will face persecution, the many hardships of verse 22.
basic.oneStory: The chapter carries the mission forward: the ascended Christ, by His Spirit and grace, plants churches among the Gentiles, and the door of faith swings open to the nations. The apostles do not merely win converts; they establish congregations with elders, so the word will take root and keep spreading toward the ends of the earth.

context.literary: Acts 14 completes the outward leg of the first journey and narrates the return. It is built on sharp reversals: belief and plot at Iconium, worship and stoning at Lystra. The speech at Lystra is Luke's sample of preaching to a purely pagan audience, complementing the synagogue sermon of chapter 13.
context.historical: Lystra and Derbe were rural towns of Lycaonia where local dialect and pagan cult ran strong; the legend of Zeus and Hermes visiting the region in disguise explains the crowd's reaction. Stoning was a real and deadly danger, and Paul later recalls being stoned once, almost certainly this event, 2 Corinthians 11:25.
context.canonical: The chapter shows the Gentile mission maturing from proclamation to church planting, with appointed elders and entrusting prayer. The report of the open door of faith sets up the crisis of chapter 15, where the terms of Gentile inclusion must be settled.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the gospel advancing through both acclaim and violent opposition, to model witness to pagans by appeal to the Creator, and to portray the apostles establishing and strengthening churches, so that God's opening of the door to the Gentiles is seen as His own work, confirmed and consolidated.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The stoning, the fickle crowd, and the specific miracles describe what happened on this journey; they are not promises that every witness will be stoned or every hearer healed. What abides is the pattern of bold, humble witness, the appeal to God as Creator and giver, the sober truth that the kingdom is entered through many hardships, and the practice of planting churches with appointed elders.

debates.question: How should the church witness to people with no knowledge of Scripture, as at Lystra?

views.label: Begin with the Creator and His kindness
views.case: The strongest case follows Paul's own method: with pagans who share no Bible, he starts from creation and God's goodness in rain and harvest, common ground that exposes the folly of idols and points to the living God. This does not replace the gospel but builds a bridge to it, as Paul does again at Athens.

views.label: Preach Christ crucified directly
views.case: Others stress that the message of salvation is Christ and His cross, and that appeals to creation are only a preface that must quickly give way to the specific good news of Jesus. Rightly held, this guards against a natural theology that never reaches the Savior, though Paul shows the preface itself is a legitimate and wise first step.

debates.bearing: Held together, the passage teaches a witness that meets people where they are, from creation to Christ, without ever stopping short of the crucified and risen Lord who alone saves.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is threaded with grace. The Lord bears witness to the message of His charis, His grace, by signs and wonders, and at the end the whole work is commended back to the grace of God. Luke names Barnabas and Paul apostoloi, sent ones, who tear their clothes rather than accept the worship due only to God. And they appoint presbyteroi, elders, in each congregation, so that the churches born on this journey would be shepherded and would stand after the apostles had gone.

typology.type: The man lame from birth, healed
typology.fulfillment: Like the healing at the temple gate in Acts 3, the restored cripple is a sign of the wholeness Christ brings, a foretaste of the healing of all creation in the kingdom.

typology.type: The living God who gives rain and harvest
typology.fulfillment: The Creator's witness in providence, mocked by idols, is fulfilled in the gospel that calls the nations from worthless things to the true and living God revealed in Christ.

typology.type: Entering the kingdom through many hardships
typology.fulfillment: The suffering road of the disciples follows the pattern of Christ, who entered glory through the cross, and points to the kingdom secured for all who endure with Him.

study.christ: Christ is the good news the apostles carry and the Lord who confirms their word of grace with signs. At Lystra the apostles refuse the honour of gods and point away from themselves to the living God, in pointed contrast to the false gods of the crowd and to Herod who died for taking such glory. The kingdom they preach is Christ's, entered by the same path of suffering He walked, and the door of faith they report opened is opened by Him, the risen Lord who is gathering the nations.
study.god: God is the living Creator of heaven, earth, and sea, who never left Himself without witness, sending rain and fruitful seasons even to nations who ignored Him. He is the one who works signs, opens the door of faith to the Gentiles, and receives the entrusted churches. The apostles guard His glory by refusing worship, and they commend their whole labour to His grace, for the mission is His work through them.

crossReferences.note: A parallel healing of a man lame from birth, another sign that points to the living God and His salvation.
God's witness in creation, so that the nations are without excuse, the ground of Paul's appeal at Lystra.
Paul recalls being stoned once, almost certainly the stoning at Lystra recorded here.
All who desire to live godly in Christ will be persecuted, echoing the many hardships by which we enter the kingdom.

study.oneStory: Acts traces the ascended Christ pressing His gospel outward by the Spirit, and here the mission matures. The apostles not only preach but plant, appointing elders and entrusting the young churches to God, so the word takes lasting root among the Gentiles. Through acclaim and stoning alike the door of faith swings open to the nations, and the report at Antioch sets the stage for the council that will secure that open door for good.

quiz.q: How did the crowd at Lystra react to the healing of the lame man?

options.text: They thought the gods had come down and tried to sacrifice to Paul and Barnabas.
options.note: Verses 11 to 13 show them calling the apostles Zeus and Hermes and preparing sacrifice.

options.text: They were amazed and wanted to make the apostles their leaders.
options.note: They were amazed, but specifically they tried to worship them as gods, which is the sharp point.

options.text: They quietly believed the gospel without any confusion.
options.note: There was great confusion; they misread the miracle as a visitation of pagan gods.

options.text: They ignored the miracle entirely.
options.note: Far from ignoring it, they were roused to attempt worship and sacrifice.

quiz.q: How did Paul and Barnabas respond to being treated as gods?

options.text: They tore their clothes and urged the crowd to turn from idols to the living God.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 record their horror and their redirection to the Creator.

options.text: They refused the worship and explained they were only men.
options.note: True, but it leaves out their positive call to turn to the living God who made all things.

options.text: They accepted the honour to gain a hearing for the gospel.
options.note: They did the opposite; they refused all such honour as belonging to God alone.

options.text: They welcomed the sacrifice as a sign of success.
options.note: They were appalled and rushed to stop it.

quiz.q: How did Paul preach to the pagan crowd who had no Scripture?

options.text: He appealed to the living God the Creator, who gives rain and harvest as a witness to His goodness.
options.note: Verses 15 to 17 build from creation and providence toward the true God.

options.text: He told them to abandon their idols as worthless things.
options.note: He did call them from worthless idols, but the heart of the appeal was the positive witness of the Creator's kindness.

options.text: He quoted the Law and the Prophets at length.
options.note: With pagans who lacked the Scriptures he appealed to creation, not to texts they did not know.

options.text: He offered no reason at all, only commands.
options.note: He gave clear reasons drawn from creation and God's providential goodness.

quiz.q: What did Paul and Barnabas do on the return journey?

options.text: They strengthened the disciples and appointed elders in each church.
options.note: Verses 22 to 23 show them establishing the churches for the long term.

options.text: They encouraged the disciples to continue in the faith through hardships.
options.note: True, but it omits the appointing of elders that secured each church's future.

options.text: They avoided the hostile cities to stay safe.
options.note: Remarkably, they returned through the very cities that had opposed them.

options.text: They abandoned the new believers and sailed home.
options.note: They did the opposite, revisiting and consolidating every young church.

quiz.q: What did the apostles report when they reached Antioch?

options.text: That God had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.
options.note: Verse 27 gives the summary: God Himself opened the Gentile door.

options.text: That they had made many disciples on the journey.
options.note: They did make disciples, but the reported climax is that God opened the door of faith to the Gentiles.

options.text: That the mission had been mostly a failure.
options.note: The report is of God's success in opening the door, not of failure.

options.text: That the Gentiles could not be reached.
options.note: The opposite is reported: the door of faith stood open to them.

questions: The crowd at Lystra swung from worship to stoning in a matter of days. Where are you tempted to build your confidence on people's approval rather than on God?

Paul met pagans with a word about the Creator's kindness in rain and harvest. Who in your life shares no faith background, and how might God's ordinary goodness become a bridge to the gospel with them?
The apostles tore their clothes rather than accept glory that belonged to God. What honour or credit are you holding onto that you should be handing back to God?
They taught that we enter the kingdom through many hardships. How does that sober word steady you when following Christ brings difficulty rather than ease?
They returned to strengthen the very churches born in hostile cities and appointed elders. Where is God calling you not just to start something but to stay and help it stand?

translationNotes.note: The crowd names Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, the chief speaker; in local legend Zeus and Hermes had once visited the region in disguise, which explains their eagerness to sacrifice.
Turn from these worthless things to the living God echoes the prophets' scorn for idols and Israel's confession of the one Creator, the standard shape of witness to pagans.
Appointed elders renders a verb that carried the sense of formal appointment; the apostles ordain local leadership in each church, entrusting them to the Lord with prayer and fasting.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 14. Footnote markers, line breaks, and note objects were skipped when building verse text. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
